entry.title: SCMS_BINARY_TO_TEXT with trailing spaces
entry.content:
Hello team , 

My requirement is to use an enhancement to send DME file from SAP to FTP server.

I get the data in format   tab_x1   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF x_linetype1.

When I try to download in desktop using below , I get correct out put and with proper trailing spaces and format.

CALL METHOD cl_gui_frontend_services=>gui_download
    EXPORTING
      bin_filesize         = filesize
      filename             = filename_string
      filetype             = 'BIN'
      NO_AUTH_CHECK        = 'X'
    IMPORTING
      filelength           = after_download_length
    CHANGING
      data_tab             = tab_x
    EXCEPTIONS
      file_not_found       = 91
      file_write_error     = 92
      filesize_not_allowed = 93
      invalid_type         = 95
      no_batch             = 96
      OTHERS               = 97.
  hlp_file_exit = filename.  

My requirement is to share the same file to FTP . In order to do this , I have to convert the file data tab_x1 into readable format using FM 'SCMS_BINARY_TO_TEXT'

TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_cont,
             line TYPE bapiascont,
           END OF ty_cont,

DATA : lt_cont type table of ty_cont

CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BINARY_TO_TEXT'
        EXPORTING
          input_length = lv_inlen
        TABLES
          binary_tab   = tab_x1
          text_tab     = lt_cont
        EXCEPTIONS
          failed       = 1
          OTHERS       = 2.

 

After which data is passed to FTP FM  FTP_R3_TO_SERVER 

CALL FUNCTION 'FTP_R3_TO_SERVER'
          EXPORTING
            handle         = lv_hdl
            fname          = lv_path
            character_mode = 'C'
          TABLES
            text           = lt_cont
          EXCEPTIONS
            tcpip_error    = 1
            command_error  = 2
            data_error     = 3
            OTHERS         = 4.
        IF sy-subrc <> 0.  

       ENDIF.

After this I get the file my folder . But my trailing spaces are not reflected. Can anyone please help me and suggest a how to keep the trailing spaces .

entry.title: Actual Rate Calculation - Event Based Posting
entry.content:
Hi All,

In my starter system, actual rate for activity is not calculating even the configuration settings have been maintained accordingly. Its a bit uregent if anybody can come up with the solution ASAP. Relevant ScreenShots have also been attached for reference

Thanks

entry.title: Financial Accounting for Intercompany Dual Service Order Scenario in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
1. Overview

I would like to show you the intercompany dual process in item-based Services including Event-Based Revenue Recognition. The screenshots below are taken from the OP2023 FP2 system. The scenario with advanced execution is valid for on-premise / private cloud only.

I start with a functional overview. Then, I show an end-to-end process in the system and close with deeper insights into the process control/system configuration.

My blog is the follow-up blog to the next two blogs:

  • New Financial Accounting for Service Management in S/4HANA Cloud by Stefan Walz (https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blogs-by-sap/new-financial-accounting-for-service-management-in-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/13489567)
  • Financial Accounting 4 Advanced Execution Service Scenario in SAP S/4HANA by Stefan Walz and Gabi Hoffman (https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/financial-accounting-4-advanced-execution-service-scenario-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/13858752)

I highly recommend reviewing these blogs first. Also, please be aware, that the process explained in this blog is different to The Intercompany Cost Allocation process explained here (https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/intercompany-cost-allocations-in-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/13442721).

 

 

2. Functional Overview

First, let’s understand the general intercompany dual service order architecture. I marked additional processes relevant only to the intercompany dual service order process in red.

Figure 1. Intercompany Dual Order - New Integrations

There are 2 additional integrations in comparison to the basic services process.

  1. Generation of a new Service ICO Order in the Execution Company B
  2. Generation of the SD Intercompany Invoice
    • 2a. SD Invoice in the Execution Company Code
    • 2b. Incoming FI Vendor Invoice Document in the Customer Facing / Commercial Legal Entity

All other process steps stay the same as in the basic service/service with advanced execution scenarios.

 

 

3. System Walkthrough

I am going to show an end-to-end service intercompany dual service order process. Please see the high-level overview below.

Figure 2. System Walkthrough Overview

Let’s have a look at each step separately.

 

3.1 Create Service Order

The Domestic US Customer (17100001) has ordered a repair. Customer-facing / Commercial Entity in the US (1710) has received the customer request and generated a service order.

Figure 3. Service Order 1: Creation

The total price to the external customer is 200 USD. The customer 17100001 is billed twice:

  • Initial Invoice – 30 USD
  • Final Invoice – 170 USD

Figure 4. Service Order 1: Billing Plan

Due to a lack of capacity in the US, the order is forwarded to the German Legal Entity (1010) for repair. You must change the item category to “SVAI - ICO Ad hoc Billing” or “SRVI – ICO Fixed Price” and define the receiver on the service order item level.

Figure 5. Service Order 1: Receiving Sales Organization

You are free to return and assign a contract with your external customer.

Figure 6. Service Order 1: Contract Selection

You have a popup with available contracts by pressing the “Contract Selection” button.

Figure 7. Service Order 1: List of available contracts

Please save the order. Below is the Financial View of the service order (and service contract if assigned) (TCode SE16n – Table Display).

Figure 8. Service Order 1: SRV-FIN Mirror Table

If you want to go deeper, you can have a look at the original CRM order tables:

  • CRMS4D_SERV_H – Order Header
  • CRMS4D_SERV_I – Order Item

For example, I had several requests to add the service organization fields to Margin Analysis reports. The field is not present in FCO_SRVDOC or ACDOCA, but you can find the field on the service order header item (CRMS4D_SERV_H).

 

3.2 Release of the Service Order

Let’s release the order item now. Please go to the «edit» mode and change the status from “In Process” to “Release”.

Figure 9. Service Order 1: Release

Of course, you could do it immediately during the order creation. I separated the steps to show the difference between them.

The first new integration will generate the second order in the execution legal entity (1010). You can see the order number on the tab “Intercompany Details” on the customer-facing legal entity.

Figure 10: Service Order 1: Link to Newly Generated ICO Service Order 2

Also, you can see the newly generated order in the transaction history:

Figure 11. Service Order 1: Transaction History

 

3.3 Intercompany Order Review / Adjustment

Please open the execution service order and add all necessary characteristics. You can see the list of mandatory things to do in the list in the left bottom corner of your screen.

Figure 12. ICO Service Order 2: Mandatory Fields

The organizational data to be checked first

Figure 13. ICO Service Order 2: Organizational Data

The second / service order is the service execution order. This functionality is available only in Private Cloud. Please see the blog https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/financial-accounting-4-advanced-execution-service-scenario-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/13858752 for details.

Figure 14. ICO Service Order 2: Data Model

You have several options for how the execution legal entity (1010) can bill the customer-facing legal entity (1710):

  • Billing on Completion
  • Billing Plan
  • Resource Related Billing (Based on DIP profile) – Itemized Billing.

In my experience, the most popular billing between 2 company codes is Resource Related Billing. So, I would use this option in my example.

Figure 15. ICO Service Order 2: Billing Relevance

Let’s release the item and save the order. Please have a look at the finance outlook table below. It gives you a great overview of the similarities and differences between 2 service orders.

Figure 16. Both Service Orders: SRV-FIN Mirror Table

Or the table below with additional comments: 

Table 1. Service Orders Comparison

After a release of the service execution order, a new execution order (plant maintenance order) is created.

Figure 17. ICO Service Order 2: Generated PM Order

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

You can release the maintenance order directly from the screen above. SAP GUI transaction IW32 can be used as an alternative.

 

 

3.4 Service Confirmation Posting (incl. RevRec)

Now, let’s post the service confirmation (Business Transaction type RMRU) in the Service Execution legal entity (1010).

Figure 18. ICO Service Order 2: Activity Confirmation

We see that EBRR posts additional documents (business transaction type TBRR) based on the DIP profile simulation and the sales price for the material customized in DIP.

Figure 19. ICO Service Order 2: EBRR Posting Based On the Activity Confirmation

 

3.5 Billing Document Request Creation

The next step is to create Service Debit Memo Request. Please use the “Create Resource-Related Billing Request” Fiori App or DP92 SAP GUI transaction code. 

Figure 20. ICO Service Order 2: Resource-Related Billing Document Generation

By pressing the button “Billing Document Request” you create the service debit memo request.

Figure 21. ICO Service Order 2: Billing Document Request

3.6 SD Outgoing Invoice Posting (incl. RevRec)

Please use the Fiori app “Create Billing Documents” or SAP GUI transaction VF01

Figure 22. ICO Service Order 2: Invoice Generation

And invoice your debit memo request.

Figure 23. ICO Service Order 2: Process Flow

 

3.6.1 ICO Accounts Receivables Invoice Posting

You can see the Accounts Receivables FI document below

Figure 24. Intercompany SD Invoice Posting

Please consider in your profitably reports the fact that fields material and product can have different values. In my case, the field “Material” is coming from the DIP/SD Invoice, and the field “Product” is attributed from the service order.

Figure 25. Intercompany SD Invoice: Difference Between Material and Product Sold Fields

 

3.6.2 Revenue Recognition based on Accounts Receivables Invoice Posting

Figure 26. EBRR Posting Based on Intercompany SD Invoice

3.6.3 IDOC Generation with Accounts Payables Posting in the Customer-Facing Legal Entity

You also can see the generated IDOC as an output item for the SD Invoice

Figure 27. Intercompany SD Invoice: Output IDOC

In the IDOC monitor (TCode BD87) you can see the generated FI document.

Figure 28. Intercompany SD Invoice: IDOC Monitor

 

3.6.4 ICO Accounts Payable Invoice Posting

The incoming IDOC has generated Accounts Payables Posting

Figure 29. Accounts Payables ICO Invoice

Here you can see one more interesting thing about attribution in Services. Our Service Contract product is “SRV_CONTRACT_CCS” is attributed in the FI document from the assigned service contract. If no assignment to the service contract takes place, the service order product will be visible in the accounts payables and event-based revenue recognition document.

The revenue recognition document in the customer-facing legal entity is posted with the EBRR closing run.

Figure 30. EBRR Postings in the Customer-Facing Legal Entity

3.7 Customer BDR / Invoice (Incl. RevRec)

The last step – we create an invoice to our final/external customer.

Figure 31. Service Order 1: Invoice to External Customer

Note: I don’t post the second invoice from the billing plan for 170 euros. 

 

3.8 Margin Analysis Overview

Let’s have a look at the whole picture from the beginning till the end.

Figure 32. Margin Analysis Process Overview (Historical Order)

Or grouped by the service order

Figure 33. Margin Analysis Process Overview By Service Document

*The second invoice for the first customer-facing service order (8*175) from the billing plan is still not posted.

 

 

4      System Preparation

In this chapter, you find the necessary information to set up the ICO Dual Service Order process in the system. This chapter covers the next areas

  1. Services Customizing – ICO Item Categories

  2. Business Partner Master Data

  3. Customizing – Intercompany Invoice (SD)

  4. DIP Profile Set Up

  5. Revenue Recognition

Note: The main assumption is that basic FI, Services and SD processes are already functional in both company codes.

 

4.1  Services Customizing – ICO Item Categories

 

4.1.1       New Hierarchical Item Categories

There are 2 item categories created for the intercompany process.

  • SRVI - Intercompany Service Item (Fixed Price)
  • SVAI - Intercompany Service Item(Ad Hoc Billing)

You can check the items in the SPRO menu. 

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Service -> Transactions -> Basic Settings -> Define Item Categories -> Assignment of Business Transaction Categories -> BUS2000112 Service Contract

IMG Object

CRMV_ITEM_MA

Transaction Code

S_AEC_66000039

Table/View/ViewCluster

CRMC_SRV_CONT_I

Figure 34. ICO Service Order Item Categories Configuration: Item Object Type Assignment

You can find the intercompany flag on the BUS20000116 Service Order / Quotation level.

Figure 35. ICO Service Order Item Categories Configuration: ICO Relevance Flag

Or parameter “ICO_RELEVANT” in the table CRMC_SERVICE_I.

Figure 36. ICO Service Order Item Categories Configuration: ICO Relevance Flag – Table View

 

4.1.2       Update Item Category Determination

The intercompany item category must be assigned to the service order type and item category group. Otherwise, it is not possible to select the item category in the service order.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Service -> Transactions -> Basic Settings -> Define Item Category Determination

IMG Object

CRMV_IT_ASSIGN

Transaction Code

S_AEC_66000040

Table/View/ViewCluster

CRMC_IT_ASSIGN

 

Figure 37. ICO Service Order Item Category Determination

Afterwards, you should be able to choose your item category in the service order.

Figure 38. Results of Item Category Determination Update

 

The item category group is derived from the product/material master data

Figure 39. Item Category Group in Material Master Data

 

4.1.3       Assign Service Transaction Types to Billing Document Request Types

Please also check the configuration below relevant for customer-facing legal entity billing. The ICO item category should be mapped to the billing document request type.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Service -> Basic Functions -> Billing -> Assign Service Transaction Types to Billing Document Request Types

IMG Object

CRMS4VC_BILL_PRTY_MAP

Transaction Code

S_AG9_66000013

Table/View/ViewCluster

CRMS4C_BILL_ITTY

Figure 40. Transaction Type Mapping to Billing Document Request Types

 

4.1.4       Assign Service Transaction Types to Billing Document Request Types

You also have to assign the ad-hoc ICO item to the billing plan.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Service -> Transactions -> Basic Settings -> Billing Plan -> Assign Billing Plan Type to Item Category

IMG Object

CRMV_BILP_BT_IT

Transaction Code

S_A2C_40000042

Table/View/ViewCluster

CRMC_BILP_BT_IT

Figure 41. Assignment of Item Category 'SVAI' to Billing Plan

 

4.1.5       Define Settings for Creation of Intercompany Service Orders

The customizing below is needed to have a list of Receiving Sales Organizations (which is assigned to execution company code) during the creation & release of the customer-facing legal entity service order.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Service -> Transactions -> Settings for Service Transactions -> Intercompany Billing -> Define Settings for Creation of Intercompany Service Orders

IMG Object

CRMS4V_ICO_DET

Transaction Code

S_ER1_83000216

Table

CRMS4C_ICO_DET

Figure 42. Setting Definition for Creation of Intercompany Orders

After the items are added you can select a receiving sales organization in the service order, item level, tab “intercompany details”.

Figure 43. Results of Setting Definition for Creation of Intercompany Orders

 

4.2  Business Partner Master Data

4.2.1       BP Roles

Please see the involved business partners with roles assignment below. No specific CRM (or other) business partner roles are required.

Table 2. Business Partner & Business Partner Roles

 

 

4.2.2       BP GL Accounts Assignments

Please double-check the correctness of your assigned reconciliation accounts via Fiori for the 1710_C – Commercial Legal Entity customer.

Figure 44. Customer Reconciliation Account Check (Fiori)

Or SAP GUI

Figure 45. Customer Reconciliation Account Check (SAP GUI)

And the vendor

Figure 46. Vendor Reconciliation Account (FIORI)

 

Figure 47. Vendor Reconciliation Account (SAP GUI)

What is also important is to have the correct account assignment group for the customer (1710_C – Commercial Legal Entity). The account assignment group is used to derive the revenue GL account (TCode VKOA).

Figure 48. Customer: Account Assignment Group (FIORI)

Or SAP GUI

Figure 49. Customer: Account Assignment Group (SAP GUI)

Note: SAP GUI transaction screens were added to explain, that it is still the same BP roles and fields we used in the past for basic SD/FI Processes.

 

4.2.3       BP Trading Partner Assignment

Please do the assignment of your business partners to trading partners via “Manage Business Partner” Fiori App.

Figure 50. Trading Partner Assignment (Fiori)

Or SAP GUI Alternative, TCode BP.

Figure 51. Trading Partner Assignment (SAP GUI)

If the field is empty in the business partner master data, then it will be not visible in the Financial transactional data. And this can influence your intercompany elimination process in the consolidation system.

 

4.2.4    Creating a Сustomer to Represent the Receiving Company

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Sales and

Distribution -> Billing -> Intercompany Billing -> Define Internal Customer Number By Sales Organization

IMG Object

V_TVKO_IV

Transaction Code

OVVA

Table

TVKO

Figure 52. ICO Customer Definition Configuration

The customer for the execution service order (in the execution company code) is derived based on the sender sales organization and this table configuration (TVKO-KUNNR).

 

4.3  Customizing – Intercompany Invoice (SD)

Automatic posting to vendor account is done by EDI. In our case, both companies are processed in the same system (& client). It is sufficient to create an IDOC. The Billing is done via SD, you can use the note https://me.sap.com/notes/31126/E as the reference to do the set-up.  

 

Transaction Code

WE21

Maintain Transactional RFC: (Choose Transactional RFC and press the create icon).

A dialogue box will open asking whether you want the system to generate an automatic

name or whether you wish to use your own name.

Figure 53. IDOC: Port Configuration

  • Port name: Automatically generated
  • Version: 4.x
  • RFC destination: NONE (as an Alternative the RFC Destination of the Client can be entered)

 

4.3.2       Output Type Maintenance

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Sales and

Distribution -> Billing -> Intercompany Billing -> Automatic Posting To Supplier Account (SAP-EDI)

IMG Object

VEDI_LOGV3

Transaction Code

WEL1

Table

EDILOGADR

Figure 54. IDOC: Supplier Definition

I use characters (1710_C, 1010_E) in the business partner. If the business partner contains digits only, the logical address must have 14 digits. If the customer number has less than ten digits, you should add leading zeros. Please take it into account, otherwise IDOC document can’t be created.

 

4.3.3       Creating a Partner Profile for both Customer and Vendor

Please set up castomer partner profile

Figure 55. IDOC: Customer Partner Profile Set Up - Overview

Figure 56. IDOC: Customer Profile Set Up - Outbound Parameters

Figure 57. IDOC: Customer Profile Set Up - Message Control

Vendor Partner Profile

Figure 58. IDOC: Vendor Partner Profile Set Up - Overview

Figure 59. IDOC: Vendor Partner Profile Set Up - Inbound Options

 

4.3.4 Configure Output Management in Fiori App

In Fiori Launchpad choose App “Output Management” and add the following rule: If receiving BP = IC Company Code use IDOC (Customizing in IMG: Cross-Application Components -> Output Control -> Define Business Rules for Output Determination)

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Cross-Application Components -> Output Control -> Define Business Rules for Output Determination

IMG Object

VEDI_LOGV3

Transaction Code

Fiori App “Output Management”

Table

EDILOGADR

Figure 60. IDOC: Output Management Set Up

You can modify the list of prerequisites. See the note https://me.sap.com/notes/3453481/E

 

4.3.5 FI-EDI: GL Accounts

In this customizing you define the cost of goods sold GL account for ICO accounts payables invoice.

Transaction Code

OBCB

Figure 61. IDOC: AP Invoice - Cost Of Goods Sold GL Account Determination

 

4.3.6 EDI-Invoice: Program Parameters

The configuration below is needed to define the document posting parameters:

  • Posting Keys
  • Document Types
  • Taxes Calculation
  • Posting Mode
    • Immediate Posting
    • Batch Input Session Creation with or without immediate processing

Transaction Code

OBCE

Figure 62. Figure 61. IDOC: AP Invoice - FI Document Parameters Set Up

 

4.4 DIP Profile Set Up

I don’t go deep into the DIP Profile details definition. However, I would like to highlight a few things.

At first, I used source “0001 Actual Costs – Line Item” and not “1005 – Intercompany Line Items” because the activity allocation happens within the execution company code only. No intercompany cost allocation takes place in this scenario.

Figure 63. DIP Profile: Source Set Up

Second, I define another billable material in my billing documents.

Figure 64. DIP Profile: Output Material Set Up

 

4.5  Revenue Recognition

4.5.1       RevRec Key Determination

The revenue recognition keys are derived based on 2 different customizing tables:

Figure 65. SPRO Menu for Revenue Recognition Keys Determination

The revenue recognition key determination configuration does not depend on the process intercompany relevance. It depends on the service order item category. If we have an execution item category (CRMC_ITEM_TYPE-OBJECT_TYPE= BUS2000192) we use the configuration below.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Controlling -> Product Cost Controlling -> Cost Object Controlling -> Product Cost by Sales Order -> Period-End Closing -> Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Derivation of Recognition Key for Execution Order Items

IMG Object

FINS_TRR_VRK_SVD

Transaction Code

S_ER9_11002295

Table/View/ViewCluster

FINS_TRR_DRK_SVD

Figure 66. Revenue Recognition Keys Determination for Execution Order Items

For all other cases (including intercompany item categories) we use the second configuration table.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Controlling -> Product Cost Controlling -> Cost Object Controlling -> Product Cost by Sales Order -> Period-End Closing -> Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Derivation of Recognition Key for Service Documents

IMG Object

FINS_TRR_DRK_CRM

Transaction Code

S_ER9_11001107

Table/View/ViewCluster

FINS_TRR_DRK_CRM

Figure 67. Revenue Recognition Keys Determination for ICO Item Categories

 

4.5.2       ICO Cost Of Goods Sold GL Account Set-Up

For many of you, it is clear how to handle the Cost of Goods Sold GL account. At the same time, I received this question from several customers already. Let me show you all three steps you need to take care of.

The GL Account Master Data

The COSS GL Account is created as the primary cost element:

  • G/L Account Type (SKA1-GLACCOUNT_TYPE)      = “P” - Primary Costs or Revenue
  • Account Group (SKA1-KTOKS)                            = “ERG” -  Income Statements Accounts
  • Cost Element category (CSKB-KATYP)                 = “1” - Primary costs/cost-reducing revenues

Figure 68. ICO Cost Of Goods GL Account Set Up – General

Figure 69. FICO Cost Of Goods GL Account Set Up - Cost Element Category

EDI Set Up

The COGS GL account determination is defined in the EDI configuration (TCode OBCB) as it is mentioned above.

 

Revenue Recognition Source Assignment

The COGS GL account should be assigned to the EBRR source:

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Controlling -> Product Cost Controlling -> Cost Object Controlling -> Product Cost by Sales Order -> Period-End Closing -> Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Maintain Settings for Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Sources

IMG Object

FINS_TRR

Transaction Code

S_ER9_11000209

Table/View/ViewCluster

FINS_TRR_SOURCE

Figure 70. Include ICO COGS GL Account as Source in EBRR

 

The source should be included in the assignment rule:

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Controlling -> Product Cost Controlling -> Cost Object Controlling -> Product Cost by Sales Order -> Period-End Closing -> Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Maintain Settings for Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Assignment Rules

IMG Object

FINS_TRR

Transaction Code

SM34 (FINS_TRR)

Table/View/ViewCluster

FINS_TRR_ASS

Figure 71. Include ICO COGS EBRR Source into Assignment Rule

You can check the GL account usage using the program FINS_TRR_ASS_RULE_DERIV_EXPL.

Figure 72. COGS GL Account EBRR Assignment Check – Selection Screen

The Report Output:

Figure 73. Figure 72. COGS GL Account EBRR Assignment Check – Report Output

The assignment rule should be assigned to the revenue recognition key.

IMG Path

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Controlling -> Product Cost Controlling -> Cost Object Controlling -> Product Cost by Sales Order -> Period-End Closing -> Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Maintain Settings for Event-Based Revenue Recognition -> Recognition Keys

IMG Object

FINS_TRR

Transaction Code

S_ER9_11000209

Table/View/ViewCluster

FINS_TRR_VAL

Figure 74. Assignment Rule Assignment to Revenue Recognition Key

entry.title: Customer deposit management process in sap
entry.content:
Hi sap friends (sap ECC 6.0)

This is the requirement from the business and we are new to this. please help us with guidance about the "Customer deposit management process" and how can we achieve this and what can we suggest the business. 

Business is asking about the below points.

Account Creation and Linking:

1.2 Set up a Deposit G/L account and sub ledger and a unique customer deposit account. Link the deposit accounts to the corresponding customer RCID (to be visible under the SFDC Hierarchy)

Financial Management:

4.2 Regular reconciliation of deposit accounts

5.1 Calculation of interest on customer deposits

5.2 Crediting interest to customer accounts

Refund Processing:

7.2 (a) Generating credit memos and (b) issuing refund cheques when appropriate

7.3 Updating deposit register/tracker and customer accounts

 

 

@raymond_giuseppi

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What’s New in SAP solutions for Three Lines, Integration with SAP ETD
entry.content:
You may recall that I previously (repeatedly!) called for a closer alignment between Cybersecurity and Enterprise Risk Management, as recommended by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) in their publication Integrating Cybersecurity and Enterprise Risk Management (NIST IR 8286). I know, I don’t have much merit advocating for something that should be common sense really...

Regardless, you may also remember a previous What’s New in SAP solutions for Three Lines detailing that NIST’s SP800-53 revision 5 and Cybersecurity Framework CSF Version 2.0 content could now be uploaded as Central Controls in SAP Process Control to help organizations enhance their IT Security & Cybersecurity controls with a best in class set of guidelines that define the baseline controls for information systems and organizations. This was released to address the Respond to Risks function.

 

NIST Risk Management Framework - Monitor Step

 

Within the NIST Risk Management Framework, one of the main components is the Monitor Step:

 

 

 


 

Purpose

Maintain ongoing situational awareness about the security and privacy posture of the system and organization to support risk management decisions.

Outcomes

* System and environment of operation monitored in accordance with continuous monitoring strategy

* Ongoing assessments of control effectiveness conducted in accordance with continuous monitoring strategy

* Output of continuous monitoring activities analyzed and responded to

* Process in place to report security and privacy posture to management

* Ongoing authorizations conducted using results of continuous monitoring activities

Source: NIST Risk Management Framework, September 24, 2024

As detailed by NIST, the objective of this step is to ensure that the measures implemented to reduce the risks – for instance the implementation of NIST SP 800-53 controls or creation of mitigating actions that were defined in the Select step, are still effective.

One of the recommended approaches here is a system-level continuous monitoring strategy. Which is exactly what SAP Enterprise Threat Management, cloud edition delivers with its real-time intelligence capability.

 

Aligning System Monitoring and Risk Monitoring

 

Unfortunately, despite NIST’s recommendation, it is undeniable that Cyber Risk Management and eEnterprise Risk Management are still not fully aligned.

In practice this mean that process owners leveraging an IT system within their process, may have a cyber risk in their risk landscape but may not have up to date information on the threat level.

Hence either underestimating it or over-evaluating it. Or even worse: completely discounting it!

As you may already know, within SAP Risk Management, near real-time risk monitoring is supported with Key Risk Indicators. These are “detectors” placed on the risks with associated thresholds that trigger notification in case a negative trend is forming and enabling risk owners to take action before it’s too late.

In order to increase the holistic representation of associated risk information and better support informed decision-making in the area of cybersecurity, SAP GRC Product Management and Engineering teams have developed an integration between SAP Risk Management and SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition to be able to record, link and track security events and respective alerts with respective risk context. This enhanced integration converts security events into risk-management language.

It does so by using SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition alerts to update Key Risk Indicators in SAP Risk Management, hence enabling continuous (and automated so effortless) monitoring and analysis of the business impact caused by security events of any connected systems directly on the associated risks.

Illustration of the integration between SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition and SAP Risk Management

 

Want to Start Using it Now?

 

If you are already using both solutions, nothing could be simpler. The supporting documentation is already published, including:

  • SAP Note 3521158 - Release Information Note of Integration of SAP Risk Management with SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition
  • Integrate SAP Risk Management with SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition section in the SAP Help Portal

 

More To Come!

 

And this is only just another step to further align the Cyber and Enterprise Risk Management worlds. More capabilities are inscribed in our roadmap and expected to come in the near future such as:

  • Cyberthreat intelligence to help ensure users stay up to date with the risk status caused by cyberthreats and move to more proactive decisions;
  • NIST content for cybersecurity by importing National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) risk management framework as a risk template or a response template.

Note: these last bullet points are only roadmap items, so all caveats around it not being a commitment, promise or legal obligation to deliver of course apply here.

So keep an eye out for the next What’s New blogs!

What about you, how does your company bring together Cybersecurity and Enterprise Risk Management? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments either on this blog or on Twitter @TFrenehard

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: SAP S4HANA Data Migration Loading Method SAP IDOCs
entry.content:
Hello All,

Today's topic is S4Hana Loading methods, we chose topic (one of the loading methods) IDOC.

What Are SAP IDocs?

SAP IDocs are standardized data containers used to transfer information between SAP systems or from an SAP system to external systems. They enable Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) and application integration, making them a cornerstone of SAP's integration capabilities.

Structure of an IDoc

An IDoc consists of three primary components:

  1. Control Record: Contains administrative data like IDoc type, sender, receiver, and status.
  2. Data Records: Hold the actual transactional or master data, organized in segments.
  3. Status Records: Track the IDoc’s journey, including stages like creation, transmission, and processing.

Common IDoc Use Cases

1. Master Data Transfer

Organizations frequently share master data like material, vendor, or customer information across systems. IDocs ensure this transfer is automated and error-free.

  • Example: Transferring material master data from one SAP system to another using IDoc type MATMAS.

2. Order-to-Cash (O2C) Automation

In the O2C process, IDocs streamline order creation, fulfillment, invoicing, and payment tracking.

  • Example:
    • Sales order creation in an external system triggers an inbound IDoc ORDERS to create the order in SAP.
    • Outbound IDocs like INVOIC send invoice details to external billing systems.

3. Procurement Integration

IDocs facilitate seamless communication between procurement platforms and SAP systems.

  • Example: Using outbound IDoc ORDERS to send purchase orders to vendors and inbound IDoc ORDRSP to receive order confirmations.

4. Warehouse Management

For logistics and inventory management, IDocs play a vital role in data exchange between warehouse systems and SAP.

  • Example: Sending delivery details to a warehouse management system via outbound IDoc DELVRY01.

5. System Integration with Third-Party Tools

IDocs integrate SAP with third-party tools like CRM, PLM, or EDI systems, ensuring consistent data flow.

  • Example: Integrating Salesforce with SAP to sync customer data using IDoc DEBMAS.

6. HR and Payroll Data Exchange

IDocs facilitate secure exchange of HR-related data with external payroll systems.

  • Example: Sending employee data via IDoc HRMD_A to an external payroll processing tool.

Key IDoc Transactions in SAP

  • WE02 / WE05: Monitor and display IDocs.
  • BD87: Reprocess IDocs in error.
  • WE20: Partner profile configuration.
  • WE19: Test IDocs for troubleshooting and debugging.
  • WE30: Define IDoc types.

Advantages of Using SAP IDocs

  1. Standardized Communication: Ensures compatibility and simplifies integration with SAP and non-SAP systems.
  2. Error Handling and Monitoring: Built-in tools help track and troubleshoot data issues efficiently.
  3. Flexibility: Configurable to meet unique business needs.
  4. Cost-Efficient: Automates repetitive tasks, saving time and resources.

Best Practices for Working with IDocs

  1. Understand Business Requirements: Know the data and processes before setting up IDocs.
  2. Leverage Standard IDoc Types: Use SAP’s predefined IDoc types wherever possible to reduce complexity.
  3. Monitor Regularly: Use monitoring tools like WE02 and BD87 to identify and fix issues promptly.
  4. Test Thoroughly: Use WE19 for testing and debugging to ensure accurate data exchange.

Technical Aspects

  • Configuration: Requires setting up partners, port definitions, and communication channels.
  • Processing:
    • IDocs are created in outbound processes via message types and processing codes.
    • In inbound processes, function modules handle the data received.
  • Monitoring: Use transactions like:
    • WE02 or WE05 for IDoc display and troubleshooting.
    • BD87 for reprocessing failed IDocs.

Advantages

  • Standardized data format simplifies integration.
  • Flexible to adapt to various business scenarios.
  • Robust error tracking and reprocessing capabilities.

Learning Tip

For a beginner in IDocs, understanding key transactions (WE30, WE20, WE19) and practicing scenarios like sending material master data or sales orders between systems is a great starting point.

Commonly Used SAP IDoc Types

While there are numerous IDoc types, here are some of the most frequently used:

 * ORDERS: Used for purchase orders.

 * INVOIC: Used for invoices.

 * DESADV: Used for delivery schedules.

 * MATMAS: Used for material master data.

Key Points to Remember:

 * IDoc Structure: IDocs have a specific structure, including segments and fields, which defines the data to be transferred.

 * IDoc Types: Different IDoc types have different structures tailored to specific business processes.

 * IDoc Processing: IDocs are processed through various steps, including inbound processing, application processing, and outbound processing.

 * ALE (Application Link Enabling): ALE is the framework used to exchange IDocs between SAP systems.

 * EDI (Electronic Data Interchange): IDocs can be used for EDI, allowing data exchange with external systems in standard formats.

 

SAP IDoc: 50 Transaction Codes You Need To Know

Find below a list of the SAP IDoc & EDI related transaction codes which I personally find useful.

  1. BD51 Maintain function modules (inbound)
  2. BD55 Maintain IDoc Conversion
  3. BD62 Define Segment Conversion Rule
  4. BD79 Maintain IDoc Conversion Rules
  5. BD87 Status Monitor for ALE Messages
  6. BDM2 Monitoring: IDocs in Receiving Sys.
  7. BDM7 ALE Audit: statistical analyses
  8. IDOC IDoc: Repair and check programs
  9. SM58 Asynchronous RFC Error Log
  10. SM59 RFC Destinations (Display/Maintain)
  11. WE02 Display IDoc
  12. WE05 IDoc Lists
  13. WE06 Active IDoc monitoring
  14. WE07 IDoc statistics
  15. WE09 Search for IDocs by Content
  16. WE10 Search for IDocs by Content
  17. WE11 Delete IDocs
  18. WE12 Test Modified Inbound File
  19. WE14 Test Outbound Processing
  20. WE15 Test Outbound Processing from MC
  21. WE16 Test Inbound File
  22. WE17 Test Status File
  23. WE18 Create Status File
  24. WE19 Test tool
  25. WE20 Partner Profiles
  26. WE21 Port definition
  27. WE24 DefaultValuesForOutboundParameters
  28. WE27 DefaultValues for Inbound Parameters
  29. WE30 IDoc Type Development
  30. WE31 Development IDoc Segment
  31. WE32 Development IDoc View
  32. WE34 Object for Display of XML IDocs
  33. WE41 Process codes, outbound
  34. WE42 Process codes, inbound
  35. WE43 Funct.module: Status record display
  36. WE44 Partner Types and Checks
  37. WE45 Forward (inbound) (V3, EDILOGADR)
  38. WE47 Status Maintenance
  39. WE55 Function Module for Path Names
  40. WE57 Assignment Messages for Appl. Objs
  41. WE60 Documentation for IDoc types
  42. WE64 Documentation message types
  43. WE70 Conversion: Basic types
  44. WE71 Conversion: Extensions
  45. WE72 Conversion: IDoc types
  46. WE73 Conversion: Logical messages
  47. WE81 Logical message types
  48. WE82 Assign Messages for IDoc Type
  49. WELI Define Status Groups
  50. WLF_IDOC IDoc Processing

Conclusion

SAP IDocs are a powerful tool for data integration and process automation. Whether it's master data transfer, process synchronization, or third-party integration, IDocs ensure seamless communication and operational efficiency. With the right configuration and monitoring, they can significantly enhance an organization's data handling capabilities.

Let's discuss more on IDOC in next blog series.

please share your feedback.

Thanks

Venkatesh Golla
